items.rationale: His most intense recent focus; spent months producing a deep video series on PoS sybil-resistance blended with BFT/longest-chain consensus, calling it an obsession.

citations.quote: Preview of the Foundations of Blockchains pipeline (proof-of-stake really drew out my obsessive side...)
Final batch (of 4) of proof-of-stake videos out now (topics: pros and cons of slashing; long-range attacks...)

items.rationale: Sustained, active research line introducing LVR as the right measure of LP loss and formalizing AMM complexity-vs-expressivity tradeoffs; still publishing here.

citations.quote: Expanded version of the original LVR paper, which fleshes out the many connections to option pricing and adds an empirical analysis
New work...formalizing the intuitive trade-off between an AMM's complexity...and its expressivity

items.rationale: Sees succinct proofs as near-magical computational compression and a key piece of web3 infra; explicitly excited to watch SNARK storylines unfold.
items.age_note: Peak enthusiasm Aug 2022; not restated with the same intensity since, but consistent with later L2/rollup framing and never reversed.

citations.quote: Excited to watch how all the SNARK-related storylines are going to play out--the suspense is killing me!
The fact that you can compress arbitrary computations in this way is shocking, IMO.

items.rationale: Frames cryptoeconomics as mechanism design with a native currency and lays out 'grand challenges' (optimal L1 incentives, monetary policy) he wants the field to own.
items.age_note: Core program articulated Nov 2022; reinforced by Jan 2023 EIP-1559 commentary and May 2023 Lecture 11.

citations.quote: Grand challenge #2: Optimal layer-1 incentives. (Holy grail: matching possibility and impossibility results as complete as in the theory of consensus protocols.)
Many problems in cryptoeconomics can be viewed as mechanism design ("inverse game theory") with access to a native currency.

items.rationale: Explicitly named DAG-based consensus the most exciting consensus development of 2022; hosted multiple seminars on Narwhal/Tusk/Bullshark and Cordial Miners.
items.age_note: Stated Oct 2022 and not restated since; ~8 months old as of analysis date but never contradicted.

citations.quote: One of the most exciting developments in consensus protocols in 2022 has been the recent advances in DAG-based consensus.

items.rationale: Newest research thread: auction theory when bids represent group preferences (DAOs) rather than individuals — a frontier he's just opened.
items.age_note: Brand-new as of the analysis date (single most recent statement).

citations.quote: New paper...showing that auction theory gets tricky when bids represent the preferences of groups (e.g., DAOs) rather than individuals

items.rationale: Foundational thesis driving his whole research lab: blockchains are virtual computers anyone can use, a new multidisciplinary field blossoming in real time.
items.age_note: Recurring thesis from early 2022 through mid-2023; durable, not time-sensitive.

citations.quote: Blockchains are (virtual) computers, not databases.
witnessing a new multidisciplinary field (spurred by web3) blossom before our eyes. There are enormous opportunities to shape this field

items.rationale: Open question whether MEV is fundamental to any decentralized smart-contract platform or merely an artifact of current designs — flags it as an unresolved grand challenge.

citations.quote: is MEV fundamental to any decentralized smart contract platform or an artifact of current designs?

items.rationale: Notes EIP-1559's incentive-compatibility breaks down at excessively low base fees — and that this is fundamental to blockspace allocation, not a fixable design quirk.

citations.quote: breakdown of EIP-1559's incentive-compatibility in the event of an excessively low base fee is fundamental to the blockspace allocation problem

items.rationale: Argues impermanent loss understates LP losses; arbitrageurs systematically extract value from LPs, quantified by LVR — a structural concern for passive liquidity provision.

citations.quote: IL is not the best loss measure...arb profits (which come at the expense of LPs) clearly do (and LVR characterizes these)
adds an empirical analysis of LVR on the Uniswap v2 USDC-wETH pool

items.rationale: Repeatedly frustrated that even smart people conflate the technology, behavior, and investment questions; sees urgent need to educate people to think clearly about web3.
items.age_note: Stated strongly April 2022; consistent with his ongoing heavy educational output through 2023, not reversed.

citations.quote: there's a lot of urgent work to do educating people to think clearly about web3. I plan to do my part

items.rationale: His recent PoS deep-dive foregrounds long-range attacks and the open game-theory of slashing as unresolved hard problems in proof-of-stake security.

citations.quote: pros and cons of slashing; long-range attacks and what to do about them
Formal game theory/mechanism design around slashing largely an open question IMO.

items.rationale: Proved permissionless consensus is fundamentally harder than permissioned, with PoW/PoS protocols forced into weaker liveness/consistency tradeoffs (CAP-style impossibilities).
items.age_note: Core result from Jan 2022; foundational and reaffirmed throughout the lecture series, but the original framing is ~18 months old.

citations.quote: permissionless consensus is fundamentally more difficult than permissioned consensus!
No proof-of-work protocol satisfies both liveness in the synchronous model and consistency in the partially synchronous model

risk_regime.rationale: Roughgarden deliberately and repeatedly refuses to take market/price positions, classifying 'which should I buy, BTC or ETH' as investment advice for a financial advisor, outside his domain as a computer scientist. His public output is entirely research and education on protocol mechanics, not asset allocation.

notes: Caveat on corpus type: this is an academic/educational corpus, not an investor's. Roughgarden expresses 'excitement' about research areas and technologies, never about tokens as investments. He explicitly partitions questions into how-the-tech-works (his lane), human behavior (social scientists), and what-to-buy (financial advisors) — see 2022-04-03 — and stays firmly in the first. Tokens: he mentions BTC and ETH only as teaching examples, never as buy/sell ideas. The one near-market observation is structural, not directional: he notes ETH is 'likely deflationary in the long run' as a consequence of EIP-1559's burn (2022-11-19), and that Bitcoin's hard supply cap drives microeconomic effects like undercutting re-org attacks as block rewards go to zero (2022-11-19) — both stated as mechanism-design analysis, not price calls, so tokens arrays are intentionally empty. Recency weighting: his most current intensities (May-June 2023) cluster on proof-of-stake design, AMM/LVR economics, and DAO auction theory; SNARK and DAG-consensus enthusiasm are slightly older (2022) but never reversed, hence marked persisted. No genuine stance reversals were found in the corpus — his views are cumulative and consistent.
